Tools & Reference
🌌 Welcome, Navigator
Celestial navigation is the art of determining your position at sea using the Sun, Moon, planets, and stars. Before GPS, every sailor crossing an ocean relied on these methods. This app guides you through each technique step by step — no prior experience required. Choose a tool below to begin.
✦

Star Finder

Identify navigational stars and planets by name, magnitude, and sky position.

☀

Sun & Moon

Calculate sunrise, sunset, moonrise, twilight times and azimuths for any location.

📐

Sextant Fix

Enter your sextant reading and get corrected altitude (Ho) ready for sight reduction.

⚓

Dead Reckoning

Project your position from a known fix using course, speed, and elapsed time.

🎯

Sight Reduction

Full pipeline: Hs → corrections → Hc → intercept → LOP accumulator → celestial fix.

🧲

Azimuth & Amplitude

Determine compass error by comparing observed vs. computed bearing of the Sun or a star.

📅

Nautical Almanac

Generate a full daily almanac page — GHA, Dec, SHA, and hourly data for Sun, Moon & planets.

💨

True Wind

Convert apparent wind angle and speed to true wind direction and speed, accounting for boat motion.

🌐

Great Circle

Compute the shortest path between two positions, with initial course, distance, and optional waypoints.

⏱

Speed & ETA

Calculate speed made good, distance run, or estimated time of arrival for any passage leg.

⭐

Celestial Fix

Automated multi-sight fix workflow: enter body, time and Ho — app computes GHA, Hc, Zn, intercept and plots the fix. Includes Polaris and running fix.

🌞

Noon Sight

Meridian passage calculator. Enter max observed altitude at local noon to get latitude directly, no sight reduction tables needed.

📏

Sextant Angles

Measure the vertical sextant angle to a lighthouse or charted object of known height to get your distance off.

🎯

Position Error

Estimate how far your dead reckoning position may have drifted from truth based on course and speed accuracy.

🧲

Magnetic Variation

Compute magnetic declination using the World Magnetic Model (WMM2025). Also build a compass deviation card for your vessel.

📍

Waypoint Converter

Convert coordinates between decimal degrees, degrees/minutes, and degrees/minutes/seconds formats. Bulk conversion supported.

📒

Log Book

Record position, course, speed, wind, and barometer at each watch change. Print as a passage log.

⚙

Settings

Set vessel name, height of eye, index error, and atmospheric conditions once — every calculator picks them up automatically.

🧭

Compass Correction

True → Magnetic → Compass bearing chain. Auto-fills from Azimuth/Amplitude and Magnetic Variation tabs.

📖 How Celestial Navigation Works

The Core Idea: Every celestial body (star, Sun, Moon, planet) is directly overhead some point on Earth at any given moment — called the Geographic Position (GP). If you measure the body's altitude angle above the horizon with a sextant, you can calculate how far you are from that GP. Do this for two bodies and where the two circles of position cross is your location.

The Tools: A sextant measures altitude. A chronometer (accurate clock) gives Greenwich Mean Time. A Nautical Almanac gives the GP of every celestial body at every moment. Sight Reduction Tables (or this app) do the math.

The Steps: (1) Observe a body, note the time and sextant angle. (2) Correct the reading for errors. (3) Look up the body's computed altitude (Hc) from your assumed position. (4) The difference (intercept) plots as a Line of Position. (5) Two LOPs crossed = your fix.

⚠️ Notice to Mariners — Limitation of Liability

Every effort has been made to ensure the accuracy of the calculations and almanac data presented in this application. However, NavBuddy is provided as a navigational aid only and must not be used as a sole means of navigation.

It is the responsibility of every prudent navigator, and a fundamental requirement of good seamanship, to employ every means available to determine the vessel's position — including but not limited to official paper charts, certified nautical almanacs, sight reduction tables, radar, depth soundings, visual bearings, and GPS. No single tool or method is sufficient on its own.

Results produced by this application may contain errors arising from software defects, imprecise user inputs, assumed-position error, uncorrected instrument error, atmospheric anomalies, or almanac approximations. Bad inputs — including incorrect time, position, sextant reading, or index correction — will produce bad fixes, and this application cannot detect or warn against all such errors.

The developers of NavBuddy accept no responsibility or liability for navigational errors, groundings, collisions, injury, loss of life, or any other accident or adverse outcome resulting from the use of, or reliance upon, results produced by this application. Use at your own risk. Verify everything.

⭐ Best 3-Star Fix Planner
Enter your position and time (e.g. twilight) to find the three stars that give optimal fix geometry — ideally ~120° azimuth spacing, altitudes 15°–65°, and maximum brightness. The geometry quality score rates the azimuth spread.
✦ Navigational Star Reference
The Nautical Almanac lists 57 selected navigational stars. Below are the most commonly used, sorted by magnitude (brightness). Magnitude scale: lower numbers = brighter. Stars marked NAV are the most practical for sights. Use the filter to find stars in a particular constellation or brightness range.
Star Mag Constellation SHA (°) Dec (°) Notes
☀ Sun & Moon Calculator
Enter your latitude, longitude, and date. The calculator will find sunrise, sunset, civil/nautical twilight, moonrise, moonset, and the moon's phase. Nautical twilight (Sun 12° below horizon) is the ideal window for star sights — sky is dark enough to see stars but horizon is still visible.
°'
Positive = North, Negative = South
°'
Positive = East, Negative = West

📐 Sextant Reading Corrector
A sextant measures the angle of a celestial body above the horizon. But the raw reading (Hs — Sextant Altitude) must be corrected for several errors before it can be used. This tool walks you through each correction to produce the true Observed Altitude (Ho), which you then compare to the Computed Altitude (Hc) from sight reduction tables.
°'
Raw sextant reading before corrections
On the arc = subtract, Off the arc = add (minutes)
Your eye height above sea surface (metres)
From almanac main correction table (altitude correction)

Correction Breakdown

Observed Altitude (Ho)
Hs (raw)
Total Correction
⚓ Dead Reckoning Calculator
Dead Reckoning (DR) is how you estimate your current position when you cannot take a celestial fix. Starting from a known position, you project forward using your course (direction) and speed over time. Every few hours you update your DR position. It accumulates error from currents and leeway, so it must be corrected with celestial fixes when possible.
°'
Starting latitude (positive = N)
°'
Starting longitude (positive = E)
0–360° clockwise from North
1 knot = 1 nautical mile/hour
Hours since last known fix
⛵ Current, Set & Drift  (optional)
Direction the current flows toward
Speed of current
Wind push off course; positive = pushed downwind

🧲 Azimuth — Compass Error by Celestial Bearing
Select a body, enter your GMT time and position, and the almanac GHA and declination are looked up automatically — LHA is derived from your longitude. Then enter your compass bearing to the body and get compass error instantly. The derived fields remain editable for manual overrides if you prefer to enter almanac values by hand.

🌅 Amplitude — Compass Error at Sunrise / Sunset
The simplest compass check — no sextant required. Select Sun or Moon, enter your GMT time and latitude, and the declination is pulled from the almanac automatically. At the moment the body touches the visible horizon, compare your compass bearing to the computed true bearing to find compass error.
Auto-fill from Almanac
°'
Edit to override almanac value
Bearing to body at moment of rise/set

Amplitude Results

Amplitude (A)
True Bearing (Zn)
Compass Bearing
Compass Error (CE)
Error Direction
📐 Compass Error — Applying the Correction

Compass Error (CE) = True Bearing − Compass Bearing. It combines Variation (magnetic, from chart) and Deviation (compass-specific, from deviation card).

Easterly error (CE positive) = compass reads less than true. Add CE to compass course to get true course.

Westerly error (CE negative) = compass reads more than true. Subtract CE magnitude from compass course to get true course.

Memory aid: "Compass to True — add East" (CADET rule) — Can Add Deviation East for True.

📐 Formula Reference
Azimuth (True Bearing to a Body)
LHA = GHA + LonE  (or GHA − LonW)
tan Z = (cos D · sin LHA) / (cos L · sin D − sin L · cos D · cos LHA)
Zn = true azimuth (0–360°T), read directly from result
CE = Zn − Compass Bearing
L = observer latitude  ·  D = body declination  ·  LHA = local hour angle
Zn is the direct true azimuth (0–360°). No east/west or north/south correction rules needed — the formula yields the correct quadrant automatically from the signs of the terms.
Southern latitudes and south declinations are handled correctly without any sign adjustments.
Amplitude (Bearing at Rise / Set)
sin A = sin Dec / cos Lat
Zn (rising)  = 90° − A  (N lat, N dec)
Zn (setting) = 270° + A (N lat, N dec)
CE = Zn − Compass Bearing
A is the amplitude angle (0–90°), measured E or W from N or S depending on declination and hemisphere.
Amplitude is only valid when the body is exactly on the visible horizon (Hc ≈ 0°). Dip and refraction are ignored at this moment.
Sign convention: N dec = body bears toward N pole; S dec = body bears toward S pole. Rising = E suffix; Setting = W suffix (e.g. E24°N → 066°T).
📅 Nautical Almanac Daily Page Generator
A Nautical Almanac daily page gives the Greenwich Hour Angle (GHA) and Declination (Dec) of the Sun, Moon, and navigational planets for every hour of GMT. These are the values you look up when reducing a celestial sight. Enter a date and this tool generates a complete daily page in the traditional almanac format.
°'
Used for twilight & rise/set times
°'
Used for twilight & rise/set times
📖 Sight Reduction Tables — HO 229 / Pub 229
Enter your AP latitude (whole degree), GHA of the body, and declination. The tool computes your LHA, selects the correct AP longitude so LHA is a whole degree, and renders the exact facing page from HO 229 — the two-page spread showing Hc, d, and Z for your latitude and the surrounding declination values. The d-correction table on the right applies the interpolation for declination minutes. Use exactly as you would the printed volume.
Nearest whole degree to your DR latitude
GHA of body from Almanac at sight time
° '
Declination from Almanac — degrees + decimal minutes
💨 True Wind Calculator
Your instruments measure apparent wind — the wind you feel because of your boat's own motion through the air. True wind is the actual meteorological wind. This calculator resolves the vector triangle: Apparent Wind = True Wind + Boat Velocity. Enter apparent wind angle as degrees from the bow (0° = head-on, 90° = abeam, 180° = dead astern). Positive angles = starboard (wind on starboard side); negative angles = port (wind on port side).
📐 Wind Triangle Reference

BEAUFORT SCALE

Force 0<1 kt — Calm
Force 24–6 kt — Light breeze
Force 411–16 kt — Moderate
Force 622–27 kt — Strong breeze
Force 834–40 kt — Gale
Force 1048–55 kt — Storm

POINTS OF SAIL

0–45°In irons / close-hauled
45–60°Close-hauled
60–90°Close reach
90°Beam reach
90–135°Broad reach
135–180°Running / dead downwind

DOUGLAS SEA STATE & WAVE HEIGHT (fully-developed open ocean)

STATE SEA STATE WAVE HEIGHT
0Calm — glassy / rippled0 m
1Smooth — wavelets0.1–0.2 m
2Slight0.2–1.0 m
3Slight to Moderate0.5–2.5 m
4Rough2.0–4.0 m
5Rough to Very Rough2.5–5.5 m
6Very Rough to High4.0–9.0 m
7High7.0–11.5 m
8Very High9.0–14.0 m
9Phenomenal>14 m

Fetch-limited coastal waters and swell-dominated conditions will differ from open-ocean values.

🌐 Great Circle Sailing
A great circle is the shortest path between two points on a sphere. Across ocean distances the great circle route can save hundreds of miles compared to sailing a constant compass course (rhumb line). However, the initial course and heading change continuously. Use the waypoint generator to break the route into manageable rhumb-line legs.
°'
°'
°'
°'
⏱ Speed, Distance & ETA
The classic passage planning triangle: Speed × Time = Distance. Solve for any one variable given the other two. The ETA mode takes your departure time and computes arrival time accounting for time zones. The multi-leg mode chains legs together for a full passage plan.
Enter each leg of your passage. Click "Add Leg" to build up the full route.
⭐ Polaris Latitude Fix
Polaris sits within about 1° of the celestial pole. A single corrected altitude gives your latitude directly — no azimuth, no tables, no time-sensitive LHA computation needed. Apply dip and refraction to Hs to get Ho, then enter it below. The app computes the precise polar distance correction (Q correction) using the current position of Polaris relative to the pole.
°'
°'
Corrected observed altitude (dip + refraction applied)
🗺 LAN Prediction — Moving Vessel
A vessel underway changes longitude continuously, so the time of Local Apparent Noon depends on where you will be, not where you are now. This tool iterates the Sun's GHA against your DR track to find the exact moment GHA = vessel longitude. Sailing east shortens the wait (you are chasing the Sun); sailing west lengthens it. The output gives you the predicted LAN time, your position at LAN, and the optimal sighting window for the parabolic fit.
°'
°'
🌞 Noon Sight — Latitude from Meridian Passage
At Local Apparent Noon the Sun reaches its maximum altitude. Your latitude is simply 90° − zenith distance ± Sun's declination — no longitude, no assumed position, and no LHA needed. Enter the date, your maximum observed Hs, and the corrections below. The Sun's declination is taken at solar noon on the given date.
To predict when LAN will occur, use the LAN Prediction card above. After running LAN Prediction, use the ↓ Auto-fill button to link the two cards.
°'
🧭 Longitude from Observed LAN Time
If you note the exact UTC time when the Sun reaches its highest point, you can derive your longitude. The Sun transits Greenwich at 12:00:00 UTC ± the Equation of Time. The difference between that transit and your observed LAN, multiplied by 15°/hour, is your longitude. This technique requires an accurate chronometer but no sextant reading.
📈 LAN Time — Parabolic Fit from Multiple Sights
The Sun's altitude curve near transit is a smooth parabola. By taking a series of sights in the 30–60 minutes before and after estimated LAN and fitting a parabola through them, you can determine the exact time of maximum altitude far more accurately than by watching for the sun to "hang" at the top. Enter at least 4 sights; ideally spread equally either side. The computed LAN time feeds directly into the longitude calculator above.
°'
°'
°'
°'
🎯 Estimated Position Uncertainty
Dead reckoning accumulates error with time. Course error fans the track sideways; speed error stretches it fore-and-aft. This calculator grows an uncertainty ellipse from your last known fix, showing the probable area where you actually are. Use this to judge when your next celestial fix is needed.
°'
°'
🧭 Magnetic Variation — World Magnetic Model 2025
Uses WMM2025 (NOAA/NCEI) high-resolution spherical harmonic coefficients to degree and order 12 to compute magnetic declination, inclination, and total field intensity at any position. Secular variation terms allow accurate use from 2025 through 2030. For critical navigation verify against the official NOAA online calculator.
°'
°'
🔄 Compass Deviation Card
Magnetic variation is the same for all vessels near a location. Compass deviation is unique to your boat — caused by on-board iron, electronics, and magnets. Enter your observed compass bearings to known true objects on several headings to compute deviation. The app interpolates the full card.
📍 Waypoint Format Converter
Convert coordinates between Decimal Degrees (DD), Degrees & Decimal Minutes (DDM — the format used in the Nautical Almanac and most chart plotters), and Degrees / Minutes / Seconds (DMS). Enter one format and the others are computed instantly.
Latitude
Longitude
FORMATTED OUTPUT

🧭 Compass Correction — Bearing Chain
Resolves the full bearing chain in either direction. True → Compass: enter a true bearing to find compass/gyro equivalents and compute errors. Compass → True: enter a compass bearing with known error to find the true bearing. All errors: E = add when converting upward (C→T); W = subtract.

🚢 Ship's Head — Gyro to Compass Deviation
Convert the ship's gyro heading to true, then magnetic, then compare to the standard compass reading on that heading to find deviation for your deviation card. Gyro Error and Variation auto-fill from the Bearing Chain calculation above — or enter them manually. Run this for multiple headings (000°, 045°, 090° …) to build a full card.

📐 Bearing Conversion Reference

Full chain (compass → true):
True = Compass + Deviation + Variation  |  True = Compass + Compass Error

Error conventions: Easterly errors are added going from Compass toward True. Westerly errors are subtracted.
Memory: CADET — Can Add Deviation East for True.

Gyro error (H/L): Gyro Error = True − Gyro. H (High) = gyro reads too high → error is Westerly (subtract from gyro to get true). L (Low) = gyro reads too low → error is Easterly (add to gyro to get true). Convention here: E = add, W = subtract when converting Gyro → True.

Ship's Head workflow: Gyro Head ± Gyro Error = True Head → True Head ± Variation = Mag Head → Mag Head − Compass Head = Deviation (for this ship's head on the compass).

Deviation: Compass Error (True − Compass) minus Variation. Varies with ship's head — use the deviation table to build a full card.

🌡 Atmospheric Conditions
Standard refraction tables assume 10 °C and 1010 hPa. Departures shift the correction — most significant below 15° altitude. Factor f = 0.28 × P / (T + 273); at standard conditions f = 1.000 exactly.
°C
Standard = 10 °C / 50 °F
hPa
Standard = 1010 hPa = 29.83 inHg

🔭 Sight Reduction
Complete pipeline: Hs → sextant corrections → Hc → intercept → LOP. Auto-ID mode: leave Body on "Auto-Identify" and the tool finds the best match. Up to 12 LOPs accumulate for a celestial fix. HoE and IE read from Settings automatically.

🎯 LOP List — Celestial Fix 0/12
    📁 Sight Sessions
    Save your current LOP set as a named session before clearing to start a new fix. Recall any past session to review or re-plot. Up to 20 sessions stored locally.
    🏃 Running Fix
    Each LOP is advanced or backtracked along your DR track to a single fix reference time you choose. This lets you combine sights taken at different times into one position. The reference time is auto-filled from your latest LOP but you can set it to any time — past or future — and all LOPs will be moved to meet it.
    All LOPs will be advanced/backtracked to this time
    True course made good
    Auto-filled from Settings
    🌅 Twilight & Daylight Schedule
    Full twilight timetable for planning star sights. Nautical twilight (Sun −12°) is the prime window — horizon visible, stars bright enough to identify. Highlighted rows show the two star-sight windows per day.
    °'
    °'
    🪐 Planetary Almanac
    GHA, declination, and visibility for the four navigational planets: Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn. SHA column allows use in the Sight Reduction tab exactly like stars. Venus is often bright enough for daytime sights.
    °'
    For rise/set & visibility
    °'
    For rise/set & visibility
    ❓ Star / Body Identification
    Shot a body but not sure which one? Enter your time, date, and assumed position — then provide Hs (raw sextant altitude), Zn (compass bearing), or both. Sight corrections (dip, refraction, semi-diameter) are applied automatically to convert Hs → Ho before matching.
    Hs alone — matches by altitude.  Zn alone — matches by bearing.  Both — combined score for highest confidence.
    1 Time & Position
    °'
    °'
    2 Observation — enter at least one
    °'
    Raw sextant reading — corrections applied below
    True bearing to body
    3 Sight Corrections
    Arcminutes — positive if on arc, negative if off arc
    Metres above sea surface
    Needed to apply the correct semi-diameter
    ⚠️
    Experimental Feature — Use with Caution
    The Auto-Identify tool is a computational aid only. It uses almanac mathematics to suggest candidate bodies based on the altitude and azimuth you enter, but it can and does make errors — particularly when sights are taken near the horizon, when atmospheric refraction is unusually high, when the assumed position is imprecise, or when multiple bodies lie close together in the sky.

    It is the mariner's sole responsibility to positively identify every celestial body before reducing a sight. Confirm identification against a sky atlas, planisphere, or the Sky Map tab. Cross-check the computed Hc and Zn against your observed values before committing to a line of position. Never rely on this tool as your only means of body identification at sea.
    🌌 Sky Map — Planisphere
    Stereographic projection of the sky from your position. Zenith at centre, horizon at rim. Altitude circles and azimuth lines every 10°. The 57 navigational stars shown, sized and coloured by magnitude. Hover any body for Hc and Zn. Use to plan twilight sights.
    °'
    °'
    Scrub time: 00:00 UTC
    Hover any body to see Hc & Zn · Alt circles every 10° · Nav stars only · Dashed line = celestial equator
    🗺 Route Planner — Multi-Leg Passage
    Build a multi-waypoint passage plan. Each leg computed as rhumb line course and distance. Enter departure time and speed for running ETAs. Current set and drift applied to effective speed per leg.
    NameLat °+NLon °+E
    🌊 Tidal Calculator — Rule of Twelfths
    Estimates tidal height at any time during a cycle using the Rule of Twelfths: tide rises/falls by 1/12, 2/12, 3/12, 3/12, 2/12, 1/12 of its range in successive hourly intervals from HW. For critical piloting always verify against official tide tables.
    Semi-diurnal ≈ 6.25 h
    🌊 Secondary Port Tidal Corrections
    Adjust a Standard Port tidal prediction to a nearby Secondary Port using time and height differences from published tide tables. Seasonal differences interpolate between the spring and neap corrections.
    1 Standard Port Prediction
    2 Secondary Port Differences (from Tide Tables)
    Differences are listed in tide tables as two pairs: Springs (MHWS / MLWS) and Neaps (MHWN / MLWN). Time differences may be given as a single value or as two (for springs / neaps).
    🌊 Current Vector Resolution
    Two modes: Solve for current — compare steered course/log speed against fixes to find current set and drift. Course to steer — apply a known current to find the heading that makes good a desired track.
    ⚠ Clearing / Danger Bearing
    A clearing bearing defines the safe limit of approach to a hazard. As long as your bearing to the mark stays on the safe side of the limit, you are clear of the danger. The tool logs each observation for watch tracking.
    ⏱ Chronometer Error Log
    Record daily comparisons of your chronometer against a time signal (GPS, radio, phone). Track accumulated error and daily rate. A constant rate is reliable; an accelerating rate indicates the instrument needs service. Error = Chronometer − Correct time (+ = fast, − = slow).
    Chronometer Record
    Date Error (s) Daily Rate Source
    📈 Barometric Pressure Log
    Log pressure every 3–6 hours. The 3-hour tendency is the key: rapid fall >3 mb/3h signals an approaching storm. Entries are corrected to sea level (QNH) if station height and temperature are provided.
    For QNH correction
    📐 Distance Off by Vertical Sextant Angle
    Measure the vertical angle between a landmark's waterline and top with the sextant. Formula: D = H / tan(θ). More accurate at close range than horizontal angle methods and requires no second observer.
    From chart — height of light above MHWS
    °'
    Degrees and decimal minutes
    📐 Horizontal Danger Angle
    Set a minimum safe distance from a hazard by measuring the horizontal angle between two charted landmarks. As long as the observed angle stays below the danger angle, you remain outside the danger circle.
    Common Light Heights (reference)
    Cape Hatteras — 59 m / 193 ft
    Eddystone — 49 m / 161 ft
    Bishop Rock — 44 m / 144 ft
    Fastnet — 54 m / 177 ft
    Cape Race — 27 m / 87 ft
    Point Loma — 37 m / 121 ft
    Lizard — 70 m / 230 ft
    Cabo de São Vicente — 86 m
    ⚖ Unit Converter
    📋 Ship's Watch Log
    Standard deck log entry. Record position, course, speed, and weather at each watch change or significant event. Entries accumulate for the session and can be exported to the Log Book.

    🕐 Local Time ↔ UTC Converter
    Convert between your local time and UTC (Universal Time Coordinated). Select your timezone offset, enter a time in either field, and the other is computed instantly. Useful for planning sights, radio schedules, and passage planning across time zones.
    Or use the "Detect Local" button below
    ⇌
    🌍 Common Nautical Time Zones
    The traditional ship's zone time system divides the world into 24 zones of 15° longitude each, labelled A–Z (skipping J). Zone letter and offset used in deck logs and official records.

    🔧 Speed Log Calibration — Measured Mile
    Run a measured distance (usually 1.0 nm between two known transits or GPS fixes) and time it. The result gives your true speed and compares it against your log reading. The double-run option (course then reciprocal) cancels tidal current so you isolate vessel speed alone.
    Run out, then return on the reciprocal course over the same measured distance. Any constant current cancels in the average.
    🗒 Celestial Plotting Sheet
    Generates a black-and-white Mercator plotting sheet centred on your assumed position. Use Send LOPs to Sheet from the Sight Reduction tab to automatically overlay your accumulated LOPs, GPs, and computed fix — all labelled. Print or download as PNG for your chart table.
    1 Sheet Setup

    History

    🔗 Additional Resources

    Official publications, websites, and tools for every aspect of marine navigation. All links open in a new tab.
    🌌 Celestial Navigation
    USNO Astronomical Applications
    The United States Naval Observatory's public portal. Provides official sunrise/sunset, moonrise/moonset, twilight times, moon phase calendars, and a complete interactive Nautical Almanac data service. The authoritative US government source for celestial body positions.
    The Nautical Site — HO 229 / HO 249
    Reference and explanation of the Sight Reduction Tables for Marine Navigation (HO 229) and Air Navigation (HO 249). Covers the complete methodology for reducing a celestial sight to a line of position using tabular methods.
    Starpath School of Navigation
    Professional navigation school offering courses, books, and free online resources on celestial navigation, weather, radar, and electronic navigation. Their free online textbooks and workbooks are some of the most complete open-access resources available.
    📚 Official Publications & Standards
    Nautical Almanac (NOAA / USNO)
    The joint US/UK official Nautical Almanac, published annually by USNO and HM Nautical Almanac Office. Contains daily pages of GHA and declination for the Sun, Moon, planets, and 57 navigational stars. The primary reference for all celestial sight reductions.
    American Practical Navigator (Bowditch) — NGA
    Nathaniel Bowditch's foundational seamanship and navigation text, maintained and published free by the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency. Two volumes covering piloting, celestial navigation, ocean passages, weather, oceanography, and electronic systems. The complete encyclopedia of practical navigation.
    Sailing Directions — NGA
    NGA's regional pilot books covering ports, anchorages, coastal hazards, currents, and navigational notes for all ocean regions. Essential pre-passage planning reference for foreign and offshore voyages. Freely downloadable as PDFs.
    USCG Navigation Center (NAVCEN)
    The US Coast Guard's navigation center covering GPS/GNSS status, DGPS, LORAN history, Notice to Mariners (NtMs), and Light Lists. Provides GPS constellation health and outage notifications — critical when verifying fix reliability offshore.
    UK Hydrographic Office (ADMIRALTY)
    Publisher of British Admiralty charts, the Nautical Almanac, tidal prediction data, and the ADMIRALTY Digital Publications suite. Also provides the NP (NP735, NP401 etc.) series of publications covering tides, lights, and radio aids.
    🗺 Charts & Pilotage
    NOAA Office of Coast Survey — Charts
    Free official US electronic navigational charts (ENCs) and raster nautical charts (RNCs) covering US coastal and inland waters. Download the latest chart editions directly or use the online chart viewer. Also home to chart correction notices and the US Coast Pilot series.
    OpenSeaMap
    A free, crowd-sourced nautical chart layer built on OpenStreetMap. Contains harbour data, buoys, lights, depth contours, anchorages, and marina information worldwide. Useful as a planning overlay, though not a substitute for official charts for navigation.
    OpenCPN Chart Plotter
    Free, open-source chart plotter and navigation software for Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android. Supports ENCs, raster charts, AIS, GPS, and a large plugin ecosystem. Widely used as a passage planning and underway navigation platform for offshore sailing.
    🌦 Weather & Routing
    Windy
    Highly visual wind, wave, pressure, and precipitation forecasts using multiple global models (ECMWF, GFS, ICON). The marine layer overlay shows significant wave height and swell direction. One of the most popular at-a-glance weather tools for passage planning.
    NOAA Ocean Prediction Center (OPC)
    Official NOAA marine forecasts for the North Atlantic and North Pacific oceans. Issues surface analysis charts, 96-hour forecast charts, and high-seas text forecasts. The standard reference for offshore weather routing in US waters.
    PassageWeather
    GFS model wind and wave forecasts presented specifically for passage planning sailors. Shows animated wind/wave overlays on a world map with a 7-day forecast horizon. Designed for offshore sailors who need a quick read on multi-day windows.
    Tides.net
    Worldwide tide prediction service covering thousands of stations. Provides tide tables, tidal graphs, and current predictions. Useful for harbour entry/exit planning where awareness of tidal range and timing is critical.
    📡 GNSS & Positioning
    GPS.gov — Official US GPS Information
    The official US government information site for the Global Positioning System. Covers constellation status, accuracy specifications, signal structure, augmentation systems (WAAS/SBAS), and policy. Authoritative source for understanding GPS performance and limitations.
    GPS Constellation Status (USCG NAVCEN)
    Live satellite health notices, scheduled maintenance outages, and GPS Notice Advisory to Navair (NANU) messages. Check this before offshore departures when precise positioning is critical — particularly useful when investigating unexpected fix degradation.
    ESA Galileo Navigation
    European Space Agency's information portal for the Galileo GNSS constellation. Covers signal types, constellation status, service levels, and interoperability with GPS. Galileo provides independent redundancy and enhanced accuracy for multi-constellation receivers.
    ⚓ Safety & Regulations
    International Maritime Organization (IMO)
    The UN agency responsible for international shipping safety and environmental standards. Publishes SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea), COLREGS (International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea), and STCW (Standards of Training). COLREGS is the essential rule of the road for all mariners.
    US Coast Guard — Boating Safety
    USCG's mariner resources covering required safety equipment, float plans, vessel documentation, distress signals, licensing requirements, and recall notices. Also provides the Light List, Coast Pilot, and Local Notice to Mariners for US waters.
    Royal Yachting Association (RYA)
    The UK's national body for recreational boating. Provides internationally recognised training courses (Day Skipper, Coastal Skipper, Yachtmaster) and publishes navigation handbooks. Their Yachtmaster Ocean syllabus is the benchmark for offshore celestial navigation qualifications.
    COLREGS.com — Rules of the Road
    Plain-language explanation and reference for the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGS 1972). Covers all 38 rules with diagrams for crossing, overtaking, and head-on situations. A useful quick-reference alongside the official IMO text.
    🎓 Communities & Learning
    Cruisers Forum
    One of the largest online communities for bluewater cruising sailors. Forums cover navigation, seamanship, equipment, passage planning, anchorages, and country-by-country cruising guides. An invaluable source of real-world experience from thousands of offshore passages.
    Noonsite
    Global cruising information database covering formalities, ports, anchorages, fuel and provisioning, local hazards, and regulations for countries worldwide. Maintained with contributions from cruisers. Essential planning reference for international passages.
    Ocean Navigator Magazine
    Publication dedicated to offshore and ocean passage-making. Covers celestial navigation, passage planning, weather routing, seamanship, and equipment. Their archive includes detailed tutorials on sight reduction, ocean current use, and bluewater voyaging strategy.
    Sailtrain — Navigation & Seamanship
    Free comprehensive online reference covering all levels of coastal and offshore navigation, from compass work and tidal calculation through to celestial navigation and ocean passage planning. Well-structured for self-study and exam preparation.
    Active Constellations
    📡 Live GPS Position
    Your device's GPS receiver provides a real-time position fix. Press Acquire Fix to get a single snapshot, or Start Tracking to log continuous breadcrumbs. Any fix can be sent to the Shared Position strip and pushed to every calculator panel.
    ⚠️
    Tracking requires the screen to stay on. Breadcrumb logging will pause if the screen locks, the browser is minimised, or the app is closed — this is a browser security limitation. For continuous background tracking, a native app is required.

    🗺 Breadcrumb Track
    ⚓ Anchor Mode — Fix Averaging ANCHORED
    Stationary vessels only. GPS at anchor typically scatters within a 5–20 m circle. Averaging 30–60 samples over several minutes collapses that scatter to a much tighter centroid — your true anchor position. The drag alarm then watches for that centroid to shift.

    Set a centre and alarm radius. If your position leaves the circle the app sounds an alarm and sends a browser notification. Grant notification permissions when prompted for alerts when the screen is off.

    ⚠ GEOFENCE BREACHED
    ℹ Usage Notes

    Permissions: Your browser will ask to allow location access the first time. Notification permissions are requested separately when you arm the geofence. This data never leaves your device.

    Accuracy: On a smartphone outdoors, expect 3–15 m. Indoors or with a weak signal, accuracy may be 100 m or worse.

    Anchor Mode: Only use fix averaging when the vessel is stationary. The drag alarm triggers when the current fix drifts beyond the averaged accuracy radius plus a margin. For reliable drag detection, average at least 30 samples (≈5 minutes).

    Geofence: The alarm radius should be at least 2–3× your expected GPS accuracy to avoid false triggers. Set it larger than the scope of your anchor chain to avoid nuisance alarms from normal GPS scatter at anchor.

    Continuous Tracking: Exported as a standard .gpx file compatible with chart plotters, OpenCPN, and Google Earth.

    Celestial Navigation: GPS is a cross-check, not a replacement. Use it to verify your celestial fix or as the starting DR position.

    📊 Fix Quality & DOP Reference
    Fix Quality
    RTK Fixed
    Centimetre-level accuracy. Carrier-phase RTK with integer ambiguity resolved. Requires RTK base station or NTRIP correction stream.
    RTK Float
    Sub-metre accuracy. Carrier-phase solution with unresolved integer ambiguity. Better than DGPS but not as precise as RTK Fixed.
    DGPS / SBAS
    1–3 m accuracy. Differential corrections via SBAS (WAAS, EGNOS, MSAS). Most modern smartphones achieve this outdoors.
    GPS (SPS)
    3–15 m accuracy. Standard autonomous GNSS fix. No differential corrections. Typical for handheld devices in open sky.
    Dead Reckoning
    Position estimated from last known fix + speed/heading sensors. Accuracy degrades rapidly with time. Treat as approximate.
    No Fix / Invalid
    Insufficient satellites or signal blocked. Move to open sky and check location permissions are granted.
    PDOP / HDOP Scale
    1251020>20
    Ideal
    < 1.0Ideal geometry — open ocean or hilltop with full sky visible.
    Excellent
    1 – 2Very low error multiplier. Reliable for celestial fix verification.
    Good
    2 – 5Suitable for navigation. Typical underway with unobstructed sky.
    Moderate
    5 – 10Usable but degraded. Sky obstruction from masts, coastline, or buildings.
    Poor
    10 – 20Low confidence. Position errors can exceed 50 m.
    Unacceptable
    > 20Do not use for navigation. Reacquire in open sky.
    HDOP affects 2-D lat/lon accuracy. PDOP includes altitude and is always ≥ HDOP. VDOP affects altitude alone. DOP values in NavBuddy are estimated from the reported horizontal accuracy figure.

    📱 Sensors

    Sensor Source
    GNSS Constellations
    Speed Over Ground
    —
    knots
    — mph | — kph
    AVG
    Heading
    —°
    device orientation
    ⚠ DEVICE TILTED
    AVG
    Altitude
    —
    metres (ellipsoidal)
    MSL ~EGM96: — m
    — ft | — fm
    AVG
    Not started
    ⚠ Device orientation requires permission on iOS 13+. Tap Request Permission to enable the compass.
    Notes

    Speed: GPS-derived speed over ground — most accurate above 1 knot. Below that, GPS noise dominates; use averaging.

    Compass: Device orientation uses the phone's magnetometer with full tilt compensation — the heading is calculated from all three rotation axes (alpha/beta/gamma) so it remains accurate when the device is held at an angle. On iOS, the system's own tilt-compensated heading is used directly. For best results, hold the phone away from metal objects and electronics. A "TILTED" warning appears if the device is more than 25° off horizontal, where magnetic accuracy tends to degrade.

    Altitude: GPS altitude from the browser Geolocation API is ellipsoidal height (WGS-84) — not mean sea level (MSL). It typically reads 10–50 m higher than MSL due to geoid undulation, which varies by location. When GPS is the altitude source, a corrected MSL ~EGM96 value is shown automatically, computed by subtracting the local geoid undulation N from the EGM96 degree-10 model (accuracy ±5–30 m globally). Barometric altitude, where available, references local pressure and is more accurate for relative changes.

    Averaging: Applies a rolling mean over the selected window. Reduces noise at the cost of lag.

    🛰 GNSS Sky View

    ⓘ These constellation selections apply to all GNSS services — Sky View, GNSS Fix, and Sensors. Disabling a constellation removes it from all calculations and displays across the app.
    Sky Plot (azimuth / elevation)
    Start tracking to see satellites
    Shapes: ● GPS ■ GLONASS ▲ Galileo ◆ BeiDou ★ QZSS + SBAS
    Fill: Solid = used in fix | Open = tracked only
    Summary
    Visible—
    Used in Fix—
    HDOP—
    VDOP—
    PDOP—
    Fix Type—
    By Constellation
    Signal Strength (C/N₀ dB-Hz)
    No satellite data yet
    About GNSS Sky View

    Browser limitation: The Web Geolocation API does not expose individual satellite data (PRN, azimuth, elevation, C/N₀) — this is restricted to native apps. CelNav simulates a realistic constellation using your current GPS position to compute true satellite geometry via almanac math, with signal strength estimated from elevation angle.

    Constellations shown: GPS (US), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), BeiDou (China), QZSS (Japan, Asia-Pacific), SBAS (augmentation). Toggle each on/off above.

    For native satellite data on Android, apps like GPS Test or GPSTest (open source) expose raw GNSS measurements via the Android GNSS API.

    🌦 Marine Weather Charts

    ⚠
    Requires internet connection. Weather charts are fetched live from NOAA's servers and are not available offline. Ensure you have an active connection before loading charts. All other CelNav features continue to work offline.
    Plot Positions on Chart
    Select a chart and press Load Chart
    About NOAA Marine Weather Charts

    Charts are fetched directly from ocean.weather.gov. Surface analysis charts are updated every 6 hours. Forecasts run to 96h. These are the same high-seas charts used by ocean-going vessels.

    Position overlays use the chart's geographic bounding box (full-ocean charts cover 0–360° longitude, 90°S–90°N) to compute pixel coordinates. The ✕ marker shows your exact position; the line shows your track.

    If a chart fails to load, it may be due to CORS restrictions. Use the direct link to open it in a new tab, or download and view offline.

    📋 Manual Sight Reduction Worksheet
    Traditional paper-style sight reduction using Pub. 229 (HO 229) or Pub. 249 (HO 249). Enter values from your sextant, Nautical Almanac, and sight reduction tables. Each mathematical step is auto-calculated. A full glossary and publication references appear below the form.
    Sights: 4 Max 12 sights reached
    Field
    📖 Field Definitions & Publication References
    Every term on the worksheet is defined below, with column / page references for the three principal sight reduction publications.
